title: Stone pattern marbling
subtitle: The foundational dropped-dot pattern in carrageenan bath marbling
excerpt: The stone pattern is the starting point for nearly all carrageenan bath marbling. Colour is dropped from a stylus or eyedropper onto the size surface in a series of dots that push each other outward into concentric rings. The result resembles a polished stone cross-section. Everything more complex, from combed patterns to bouquets, begins here.
sourceNotes: Charles Woolnough, The Art of Marbling (1853), describes the dropped-colour stone pattern as the foundational marbling form. The pattern is documented in Franz Weisse, Das Buntpapier (1892), and in the marbling manuals archived at the Bibliothèque nationale de France. All pre-1928.

content.text: is the first pattern every marbler learns because it demonstrates the fundamental physics of the craft: colours dropped onto the

surface float and spread outward, pushing any colour already on the surface into a ring. A second colour dropped in the centre of the ring pushes it further out and forms its own ring inside it. Three to five colours dropped in sequence produce a pattern of nested rings resembling polished agate. Paper pre-treated with
solution is then laid on the surface to capture the pattern.

items.qty: Enough to fill the tray to 4 to 5 cm depth
items.name: Carrageenan size, mixed and rested at room temperature for at least 12 hours
Marbling tray (larger than the paper you intend to marble)
Marbling paints or acrylic inks prepared to marbling consistency (ox gall or Aquadrops added for spreading)
Eyedropper or small brush for applying paint to the size

items.qty: Small amount in a flat tray
items.name: Alum solution (15 g alum dissolved in 1 litre warm water)
Smooth paper (cartridge or text-weight bond), pre-coated with alum and fully dry
Newspaper strips (for skimming the size surface between sheets)

content.text: Preparing the paper and checking the size
Coat each sheet of paper lightly with alum solution using a wide soft brush. Let dry flat. The alum must be completely dry before marbling or the pattern will not adhere.

Test the size surface by dropping a small dot of one colour from about 10 cm above the surface. It should spread into a round disk 2 to 4 cm across. If it sinks immediately, the size is too thin or the paint mixture needs more ox gall. If it does not spread at all and sits as a lump, the paint is too thick or the size too heavy.
Skim the size surface with a strip of newspaper to remove dust and any dried film. Discard the strip.
Drop the first colour across the surface in a scattered pattern: about one drop every 5 cm. Let each drop spread before adding the next.

Drop the second colour into the centre of each first-colour disk. It will push the first colour outward into a ring. Continue with the third, fourth, and fifth colours in the same way, each one dropping inside the previous ring.

When satisfied with the coverage, lay the alum-coated paper on the surface in a single smooth motion: hold the sheet by two edges, bow it gently downward so the centre touches first, then lower the edges. Lift after 30 seconds by peeling from one corner.
Rinse the sheet under a gentle stream of cold water to remove the size. Let dry flat or hang.

attrs.intro: Problems in colour spreading and pattern transfer.

items.fix: Add one or two drops of ox gall to the paint and stir. Test on the size surface before covering the tray.
items.cause: Paint not prepared with enough spreading agent (ox gall or Aquadrops), or size too thin.
items.symptom: Colours sink rather than float on the size

items.fix: Allow alum-coated paper to dry for at least 1 hour before marbling. Use 15 g alum per litre.
items.cause: Alum not fully dry on the paper, or alum solution too weak.
items.symptom: Pattern does not transfer to the paper, paper lifts with no colour

attrs.heading: Common problems with the stone pattern
